items.title: US 10-Year Approaching 5% — Markets Can No Longer Ignore Inflation
items.idea: Long-end US Treasuries are breaking down globally (US, Japan, UK) as inflation runs persistent and oil spikes from the Iran conflict. WealthVest argues the current cycle is uniquely strange: housing is historically weak, job growth anemic, consumer confidence recessionary — yet markets have ignored all this because AI-capex-driven earnings growth overwhelmed everything. But if 10yr hits 5%, cost of capital matters for data centers, housing prices decline broadly, and discretionary spending of the top-decile consumer (the only consumer propping this up) gets hit.
items.angle: This is the bond vigilante moment that has been predicted for two years — and the Iran war is finally providing the catalyst. Data center capex financing assumptions modeled at 4.5% yields look very different at 5%+. The hyperscaler CapEx boom is not rate-neutral.
items.tweetA: The 10-year is approaching 5%. Last time this happened markets eventually broke. The difference now: AI capex giants have levered up at 4.5%. Refinancing risk meets data center construction. Nobody is doing this math in the NVDA bull case.
items.tweetB: Iran war doing what trade war couldn't: finally breaking the bond market's back. WealthVest lists every force pushing yields higher simultaneously. The 'Fed is always dovish' trade is ending. The question is whether equities notice before or after the 10yr hits 5%.

charts.desc: Long-end Treasury yield breakout chart — yields breaking above key resistance; same pattern visible in Japan and UK simultaneously.

items.title: NY Manufacturing 19.6 vs 7.2 Expected — New Orders Strongest Since Dec 2022, Prices Paid 2022 Highs
items.idea: May NY Fed manufacturing index came in at 19.6 vs 7.2 expected (prior 11) — the biggest beat in recent memory. New orders rose to highest since December 2022. But the stagflation alarm: prices paid jumped 11.6 points to highest since July 2022; prices received up 10 points to most since August 2022. Also: Viking Holdings 92% booked for 2026, with 2027 already 38% booked (15% capacity increase). Klarna reports consumer delinquency rates stable. April IP at +0.7% (est 0.3%), strongest since February 2025.
items.angle: The simultaneous surge in new orders AND prices paid is the definition of stagflation in manufacturing. The Fed cannot cut into this (inflation accelerating) and cannot easily hike (markets levered to rate-cut narrative). Viking 38% booked for 2027 tells you something about wealthy consumer durability.
items.tweetA: NY manufacturing 19.6 vs 7.2 expected. But buried in the print: prices paid jumped 11.6 points — highest since July 2022. New orders at Dec 2022 highs. Inflation acceleration and demand strength at the same time. The Fed has no clean move here.
items.tweetB: Manufacturing orders booming. Prices paid at 2022 highs. Viking cruises 92% booked for 2026, already 38% booked for 2027. Klarna delinquencies stable. The economy is not breaking — but inflation is re-accelerating. That is the exact setup that takes the most money from the most people.

charts.desc: Charts inside article — worth opening for IP and price series breakdowns.

items.title: Trump Outplayed in Beijing: Lower Chair, Putin Comparison, and Taiwan Ambiguity
items.idea: Peter Frankopan details how the Trump-Xi summit was a comprehensive diplomatic win for China. Trump was seated in a smaller, lower chair. Xi admitted Trump to the Zhongnanhai garden, noting it was reserved for 'very few — Putin was one'; Trump replied 'Good, I like it.' Xi apparently called the US 'perhaps a declining nation' — and Trump posted it on Truth Social. Most consequential: Trump refused to confirm the US would defend Taiwan, said China has 'had it for thousands of years and we're going to get it back.'
items.angle: The Taiwan statement is the most significant geopolitical signal in this briefing. If the US president will not confirm commitment to Taiwan's defense, the deterrence calculus changes. That has direct implications for $TSM and the entire semiconductor supply chain built around Taiwan's continued autonomy.
items.tweetA: Trump in Beijing won't say if the US would defend Taiwan. His explanation: 'China has had it for thousands of years.' I don't know if that's negotiating posture or a policy shift. But $TSM shareholders should probably find out before the next earnings call.
items.tweetB: Xi told Trump the US is 'perhaps a declining nation.' Trump posted it on Truth Social. China released the clip where Trump asks if Putin got garden access. Xi: 'Yes, very few. Putin was one.' Trump: 'Good, I like it.' China did not need to fire a single shot in this summit.

charts.desc: No charts — narrative essay.

items.title: Bears Missed the Narrowing In Rally — The 2000 Druckenmiller Analog in Real Time
items.idea: Paulo Macro admits he 'had this' — he predicted a final large-cap Narrowing In run in February (drawing parallels to Jan 2000 Nasdaq vs. Russell divergence and the 1972 Nifty Fifty broadening-then-narrowing cycle) but didn't trade it. His reasoning: he couldn't believe a Narrowing In rally would happen 'in the teeth of an energy crisis worse than 1973.' Positioning now: CTAs reversed course 'in historic fashion,' call buying is 'off the charts,' vol-control funds still have room to run to max long.
items.angle: This is the meta-lesson of the cycle: the framework was right (Nifty Fifty analog, final largecap jam), the trade was missed. Druckenmiller bought hours from the March 2000 top. The question now is whether we are in the post-Druckenmiller window where the decline follows shortly.
items.tweetA: Druckenmiller bought the Nasdaq hours from the March 2000 top. Paulo Macro 'had the framework' for exactly this Narrowing In rally in February and didn't trade it. CTAs reversed 'in historic fashion.' This looks like the final jam — not the beginning of a new bull run.
items.tweetB: Broadening Out is a late-stage bull narrative in every major top: 1972 before Nifty Fifty peak, Jan 2000 before Nasdaq peak, 2021. Pattern: smallcaps briefly outperform, then largecaps rip one last time. We just did the smallcap phase. Know where you are in the sequence.

charts.desc: Multiple charts: Nasdaq vs Russell Jan 2000, S&P 1970-74 Nifty Fifty era, IBM/NYSE proxy through Q1 1973 top. Historical cycle overlay.

items.title: Mark to Myth Goes Public — Private Equity's Valuation Playbook Runs Through Hyperscalers Now
items.idea: Paulo Macro's title reveals a second thesis: 'Private Equity Invented the Playbook. Hyperscalers Now Run It.' The argument — 'mark to myth' — is that hyperscalers are applying PE-style aspirational valuation conventions to their AI infrastructure in public markets, where the opacity of private marks is unavailable. The Narrowing In rally is being justified by narrative construction: $1T committed capex requires revenue buildout that the bulls project as certainty, while the depreciation clocks are already running.
items.angle: This is the sharpest framework for the current AI infrastructure cycle: PE math applied to listed companies. In PE, you can carry assets at model. In public markets, earnings seasons force reality. The depreciation clocks on $200B+ in AI infrastructure spending are already running.
items.tweetA: Private equity marks assets to myth. Hyperscalers have learned to do it publicly. When Microsoft projects GPT-5 will generate $10T in productivity gains, they're using KKR's Excel sheet. The difference: PE can hide marks. Public markets eventually can't.
items.tweetB: The 'AI capex is different this time' argument is PE modeling entering public markets. DCFs on AI infrastructure require: a) revenue materializes, b) at projected margins, c) within the depreciation window. The bear case: 0 for 3. The depreciation clocks are already running.

charts.desc: Article continues behind paywall — charts likely inside.

items.title: 7 Simultaneous Headwinds for Bonds — Tightening Framework Bearish Gold, Euro, Yen
items.idea: Paul Podolsky makes a 7-point bearish case for bonds: (1) nominal growth ~7% while yields below 5%; (2) households don't buy bonds; (3) large government deficits; (4) tariffs declared illegal — revenue must be refunded; (5) US in war with 'tactical victory but strategic defeat,' requiring more spending; (6) new Fed chief reluctant to hike, boosting long-end risk premium; (7) capex boom requires debt. Cross-asset: US inflation ~4%, European ~3%, both heading higher on Iran/oil. Elevated oil = bad for euro and yen, good for Brazil and Norway. Fed tightening = dollar bullish, bearish for gold/silver/copper.
items.angle: The tariff-declared-illegal point is underappreciated: if courts force revenue refunds, the fiscal deficit expands by the amount already counted as revenue. The gold short thesis from tightening is also contrarian vs. consensus long gold.
items.tweetA: Podolsky lists 7 simultaneous reasons bonds fall further. The underpriced one: tariffs were declared illegal. The government has to refund revenue it already counted. Run the deficit math again — you're probably short by more than you think.
items.tweetB: US inflation 4%, European 3%, both heading higher on Iran oil. Tightening is dollar bullish, bearish gold, silver, copper. Brazil and Norway win on oil exposure. The entire consensus trade — Fed cuts, everything rallies — requires oil to fall. Oil is up 10% this week.

charts.desc: S&P vs Fed Funds rate 2022 — stocks fell ~15% during last tightening cycle.

items.title: Wolfspeed $WOLF: From $0.50 to $70 — SiC MOSFETs Solving the MV Transformer Backlog
items.idea: Wolfspeed went from near-bankruptcy ($0.50 stock, Chapter 11) to $70 on a single product: a 10kV SiC MOSFET with no comparable competitor. Medium Voltage transformers have 3-year backlogs (confirmed by Reuters), choking datacenter buildouts. Wolfspeed's SiC devices replace iron-core MV transformers with solid-state switching converters, eliminating dependency on the supply-constrained iron-core hardware. Company still runs a GAAP net loss of ~$120M.
items.angle: The MV transformer backlog is the supply chain bottleneck getting the least airtime in the datacenter narrative. If one company with a unique SiC transistor can bypass a 3-year supply chain constraint for datacenter power infrastructure, the re-rating is explainable even at a GAAP loss.
items.tweetA: $WOLF went from $0.50 (Chapter 11) to $70. The reason: a 10kV SiC transistor that exists nowhere else. Medium voltage transformers have 3-YEAR backlogs choking datacenter buildouts. Wolfspeed replaces the iron core with solid-state switching. This is the bottleneck story.
items.tweetB: Everyone knows the datacenter power bottleneck is real. Not everyone knows the specific chokepoint: MV transformers, 3-year backlog, limited US manufacturing. $WOLF figured out how to bypass them entirely with SiC. Still losing $120M/year GAAP — but the product is irreplaceable right now.

charts.desc: SiC MOSFET product photo + Reuters MV transformer backlog screenshot.

items.title: Semtech CopperEdge: <2W Per Cable End at 200G/Lane — 10x Power Advantage Over Credo AECs
items.idea: Semtech is developing analog copper interconnects for AI scale-up that achieve <2W or ~1.25 pJ/bit at 1.6T — versus Credo HiWire AECs at ~20W (~12.5 pJ/bit). Key: no ADC/DAC/DSP, purely analog equalization boosting weakened PAM4 high-frequency components. This also delivers 10s of picoseconds latency (vs. nanoseconds with DSPs), critical for NVLink/InfiniBand scale-up domains.
items.angle: At 1.6T scale across thousands of connections per rack, a 10x power reduction per cable end reshapes the power budget of AI clusters. The hyperscalers designing next-gen HGX systems are running power budget calculations right now — if this product is real at scale, it belongs in those calculations.
items.tweetA: Semtech claims 1.25 pJ/bit at 1.6T with copper, no DSP. Credo does 12.5 pJ/bit. If this holds at scale, the power math for AI scale-up clusters changes by an order of magnitude — and every hyperscaler rethinks their interconnect stack. $SMTC
items.tweetB: No ADC. No DAC. No DSP. Pure analog equalization at 1.6T, <2W per cable end vs ~20W for competing AECs. And picoseconds of latency vs. nanoseconds. If Semtech CopperEdge delivers what it claims, this is the biggest quiet win in AI infra in months.

charts.desc: No charts in email — technical description only.

items.title: MCTS vs Naive RL: The Credit Assignment Problem Across 100K Tokens
items.idea: Eric Jang (former Google DeepMind, former VP AI at 1X Technologies) explains why AlphaGo's Monte Carlo Tree Search is architecturally superior to naive policy gradient RL: MCTS provides 'a strictly better action every single move, giving you a training target that sidesteps the credit assignment problem.' Naive policy gradient RL 'has to figure out which of the 100k+ tokens in your trajectory actually got you the right answer.' Jang concludes 'the way humans learn is surely closer to MCTS than naive policy gradient.'
items.angle: This is the key bottleneck in scaling LLM reasoning. RLHF and GRPO work but are sample-inefficient because they cannot identify which of 100K tokens earned the reward. The companies that solve the credit assignment problem will have a step-function advantage in reasoning model training.
items.tweetA: Training a reasoning LLM: you get one reward signal for 100,000 tokens of output, and no idea which tokens earned it. AlphaGo got a reward for every single move, and a suggestion for what to try next. This is why scaling RL for LLMs is so much harder than scaling RL for games.
items.tweetB: DeepMind's AlphaGo used MCTS as a training signal — it suggests a strictly better move at each step. RLHF/GRPO for LLMs has to backwards-distribute a single reward across 100K tokens. No wonder reasoning models plateau. The architecture we're using to teach LLMs to think may be the wrong one.

charts.desc: 158-minute video with chalkboard illustrations. Watch on YouTube — chalkboard content is the key asset.

items.title: Autoresearch Loops: LLMs Can Run Experiments, But Cannot Choose What to Ask
items.idea: Eric Jang ran an autoresearch loop on his AlphaGo project — LLMs automating the research cycle. Key findings: LLMs CAN automate implementing and running experiments and optimizing hyperparameters. LLMs CANNOT automate 'choosing the right question to investigate next' and 'escaping research dead ends.' This maps precisely onto the intelligence explosion debate: automated AI research labs can accelerate execution-phase work but hit a wall at genuine scientific direction-setting.
items.angle: The intelligence explosion narrative requires that AI can automate AI research end-to-end. Jang's data says: yes for implementation, no for direction. A lab where AI runs 10,000 experiments per day but humans pick which ones to run is fast, not recursive self-improvement. The bottleneck is question-selection.
items.tweetA: Eric Jang ran an LLM autoresearch loop on AlphaGo. LLMs are great at running experiments and optimizing hyperparameters. They cannot choose the right question to investigate. And they cannot escape dead ends. That is the exact crux of the intelligence explosion debate.
items.tweetB: The intelligence explosion assumes AI can automate AI research. Jang's empirical data: yes for implementation, no for direction. A lab where AI runs 10,000 experiments but a human picks which 10 are worth running — that's fast, but it's not recursive self-improvement. The bottleneck is question-selection.

charts.desc: Chalkboard explanations in YouTube video — see timestamp 02:22:16 for Automated AI Researchers section.

items.title: Intel-SMIC JV: The Unexplored US-China AI Infrastructure Deal Nobody Is Discussing
items.idea: Michael Parekh proposes an Intel-SMIC (China's version of TSMC) JV as a pragmatic US-China tech cooperation step ahead of September Xi-White House visit. Logic: Intel is up 200%+ post-USG equity investment, Elon partnership, Apple partnership consideration — and the US government already holds Intel equity that could be used to structure a SMIC JV. Additional: roll back FCC Brendan Carr drone/cellular modem import curtailments that harm US industries dependent on Chinese hardware they cannot domestically manufacture.
items.angle: The US government's Intel equity stake is an underutilized diplomatic lever. If an Intel-SMIC JV becomes part of pre-September negotiations, $INTC has geopolitical upside optionality in zero sell-side models. An idea floating in one newsletter — if it moves into policy circles, the timeline compresses fast.
items.tweetA: Unexplored US-China idea: Intel-SMIC JV structured using the US government's existing Intel equity stake. SMIC is China's TSMC. Give them a legal chip-manufacturing partnership outside Huawei. Nobody is talking about this. $INTC has geopolitical upside completely unpriced.
items.tweetB: The FCC has been blocking DJI drone imports and Chinese cellular modem chips. These are products US companies cannot domestically manufacture. We are banning components we depend on for shallow political reasons. Rolling that back before September's Xi visit costs nothing and buys meaningful goodwill.

charts.desc: No charts — analytical framework.

items.title: China No Longer Copying — It Is Leading on EVs, Batteries, Robots, Drones
items.idea: Parekh makes a strong empirical claim: the 'China is behind' frame from 2021-2023 is now inaccurate across most hardware categories. China leads in: BYD (EVs), CATL (batteries — world's largest), Unitree (humanoid robotics at scale), DJI (drones + cameras). US business leaders and VCs who visited mainland China 'have come back amazed.' US still leads on frontier AI and most advanced process semiconductors. But categorical US hardware leadership no longer holds across physical technology.
items.angle: US investors systematically underestimate Chinese hardware leadership because exposure is legally difficult and politically unpopular. The companies bridging this asymmetry — JV structures, component suppliers, industrial supply chain intermediaries — may capture an edge invisible from a NASDAQ-only portfolio.
items.tweetA: US business leaders and VCs who visited mainland China have been coming back amazed. The story has flipped. China is ahead on EVs, batteries, humanoid robots, drones. Still behind on frontier AI and chips. But 'China is copying US tech' is no longer empirically accurate in 2026.
items.tweetB: Unitree makes humanoid robots at scale. CATL dominates global battery manufacturing. BYD builds EVs faster than Ford can respond. DJI owns the drone market. Meanwhile the US blocks imports of these products. At some point blocking your competitor's hardware also blocks your own industrial progress.

charts.desc: No charts — policy and market structure analysis.

items.title: Meta's Neural Wristband: The Underrated AI UI Layer That Could Work on Any Device
items.idea: Parekh argues Meta's Ray-Ban Display upgrade's most important feature is not the glasses — it is the neural wristband controller (from a ~2-year-old Meta acquisition) that uses finger-movement detection for neural handwriting input with no visible touch surface. He proposes this technology could become a cross-device AI UI/UX platform independent of the glasses, potentially giving Meta an entry point into iPhone and Android control surfaces. Proposed structure: spin out as majority-owned Meta subsidiary.
items.angle: If the wristband works on any device, Meta gains a data collection layer on Apple and Google hardware — something neither company would normally allow. The platform monetization angle is more interesting than the glasses angle, which is exactly why Apple will resist it.
items.tweetA: Everyone is focused on Meta's AI glasses. Parekh says the real innovation is the wristband: neural handwriting via finger movement, no touch surface, acquired two years ago. If this becomes a cross-device controller — Meta just got a data layer on hardware it does not own.
items.tweetB: Meta acquired a neural wristband company years ago. The Ray-Ban Display launch is finally deploying it. Parekh's thesis: spin it out, let it work on any device. If it works on iPhones, Apple has Meta's fingerprint on every AI-connected interaction. Probably exactly why Apple won't allow it.

charts.desc: No charts. Video segment at linked Substack post.

items.title: NEAR Intents: $18B Crosschain Volume Total, $5B Since February 2026 Alone
items.idea: NEAR Protocol's Intents (crosschain interoperability system) has processed $18B in total crosschain volume, with $5B arriving since February 2026 — a sharp acceleration. Architecture: chain signatures + competing solvers enable ETH-to-BTC swaps without manual bridging. Integrated by Infinex, Kyber, Ledger, LI.FI. NEAR token up ~50% in 90 days. New tokenomics wire Intents and NEAR AI revenues into $NEAR buybacks. NEAR AI layer (TEE-based private inference, Agent Hosting, Agent Market) targets agentic economy.
items.angle: The chain abstraction thesis now has real velocity data — $5B in 3 months is not retail speculation. The buyback mechanism creates a direct link between protocol usage and token value that most L1s lack. AI agents as market participants is the highest ceiling for the NEAR AI layer.
items.tweetA: NEAR Intents just hit $18B in crosschain volume, with $5B of that in the last 3 months alone. ETH to BTC without a bridge. Integrated by Kyber, Ledger, LI.FI. The chain abstraction narrative finally has data behind it. $NEAR up 50% in 90 days — look at what is driving it.
items.tweetB: The agentic economy needs rails for AI agents to move money across chains securely. NEAR is building exactly that: private inference via TEEs, agent hosting, an Intents-powered exchange for agents. If AI agents become market participants at scale, NEAR positioned itself as the settlement layer.

charts.desc: Cumulative NEAR Intents crosschain volume chart — up and to the right with visible acceleration since Feb 2026.

items.title: Wall Street Lobbying to Regulate Hyperliquid: CME and ICE Push US Regulators
items.idea: Bloomberg reports that CME Group and ICE are urging US regulators to enforce existing financial laws against Hyperliquid — the decentralized perpetuals exchange that has grown rapidly and eaten into traditional derivatives volume. This is established financial infrastructure using regulatory channels as competitive tools against a crypto DEX.
items.angle: When CME and ICE spend political capital lobbying against a crypto platform, it signals that platform has become large enough to genuinely threaten their revenue. This is simultaneously the clearest bullish indicator for Hyperliquid's relevance and the clearest bearish indicator for its regulatory outlook.
items.tweetA: CME and ICE are lobbying regulators to enforce existing laws against Hyperliquid. This is the clearest possible signal: Hyperliquid has gotten big enough to genuinely threaten traditional exchange revenue. Regulation as competitive moat — Wall Street's oldest playbook. $HYPE
items.tweetB: The most bullish data point for Hyperliquid is that CME and ICE are scared enough to call their congresspeople. The most bearish: they have more lobbyists and the relationship map. Regulatory capture vs decentralization — we will find out which wins first.

charts.desc: No charts — news item.

items.title: BTC $79K, ETH $2.2K, Oil +10% on the Week — Markets Digest US-China Summit Disappointment
items.idea: Oil futures jumped 10% this week (Iran conflict). BTC fell 2.3% to $79,214 (down 0.8% 7-day). ETH fell 3.2% to $2,221 (down 3.1% 7 days). Total crypto cap $2.64T, down 2.8% on the day. Market disappointed US-China summit ended with no major announcement. Strategy preparing $1.5B bond buyback and contemplating BTC sales to fund dividends. Kraken cutting 150 staff ahead of planned IPO, seeking $20B valuation in fresh private capital raise.
items.angle: BTC at $79K while oil is at $100+ is a thesis test: if BTC is a hard-money inflation hedge, it should be performing strongly when oil spikes from geopolitical conflict. It is not. BTC is trading like a risk asset.
items.tweetA: Oil up 10% this week. BTC down. Crypto market down. If the 'hard money / inflation hedge' BTC narrative were working, you'd expect the opposite. At $79K with oil over $100, BTC is behaving like a risk asset, not a commodity. That is a thesis-testing moment worth tracking.
items.tweetB: Strategy: $1.5B bond buyback, now considering selling BTC. Kraken: 150 layoffs before IPO. US-China summit: no announcement. Oil +10%. If the market is absorbing all of this and holding $79K on BTC — that is either genuine resilience or a lag that resolves violently.

charts.desc: Market snapshot table — BTC, ETH, total crypto cap with 24hr and 7d changes.

items.title: 2031: Goldman Goes Fully Agentic — The Full Scenario of How Finance Gets Automated
items.idea: Rupak Ghose writes a detailed 2031 scenario of AI disruption across the stock market information ecosystem. Key markers: Claude Opus 4.6 (Feb 2026) enables DCF modeling in minutes → FactSet/S&P Global shares drop 10%. Goldman announces 'OneGS 3.0': 'Our human assembly lines will become more digitized, digital agents will be our robots.' Goldman goes fully agentic → PE multiple doubles as investors value it as a tech company. Mamdani Congressional hearings follow. By end 2028: hedge fund + sell-side headcount down 25%. Norges Bank reduced trading costs by NOK 5bn using AI. By 2031: AI agents gaming IR systems and starting to share confidential information.
items.angle: The Goldman PE multiple re-rating as a tech company is the actionable angle: moving from 1x book to 20x+ earnings multiples is the real AI trade hiding inside the largest market-cap financial institutions. That multiple expansion is invisible in current valuation models.
items.tweetA: Goldman Sachs announces it is replacing all human investment researchers with AI agents. Stock jumps 20%. Then keeps going. Investors start valuing it as a tech company, not a bank. The PE multiple doubles. This is the real AI trade nobody prices: Goldman Sachs as SaaS platform.
items.tweetB: Rupak Ghose's 2031 scenario: AI agents are gaming investor relations systems, extracting confidential info through clever prompting. Congress holds hearings. The endpoint of fully agentic finance is machines talking to machines — and historically, that has not been stable.

charts.desc: No charts — narrative scenario. Long-form piece worth reading in full.

items.title: US Large-Cap Sell-Side Stock Traders: Down 95% Since the Dot-Com Bubble — Research Is Next
items.idea: US large-cap sell-side stock traders have declined 95% since the dot-com bubble as banks automated. Research analysts have been more insulated — but that is now changing. The same hedge funds driving buy-side growth (Balyasny 95% AI adoption, Walleye 100% AI, Citadel working with Anthropic) are the fastest AI adopters. When these firms go agentic, the sell-side loses its biggest remaining clients simultaneously, triggering a second wave of sell-side cuts larger than the first.
items.angle: The buy-side growth that sustained sell-side research for the last decade is now the vector for sell-side collapse. When your largest clients replace junior analysts with AI agents, the sell-side's volume and fee base collapses faster than internal headcount cuts can compensate for.
items.tweetA: US sell-side stock traders: down 95% since the dot-com bubble. That happened quietly over 20 years. Now it is research analysts' turn. The buy-side headcount is growing — until hedge funds go agentic. When that happens, sell-side loses its biggest clients. This is Wave 2.
items.tweetB: Balyasny: 95% AI adoption. Citadel working with Anthropic. Walleye: 100% AI. These are the firms that drive sell-side research volumes. When your top client goes agentic, your business model breaks — regardless of your own internal AI adoption. The sell-side is about to lose its anchor tenants.

charts.desc: No charts — analytical narrative.

items.title: April IP +0.7%, 2x Expectations — Business Equipment at 7-Year High, But Real Y/Y Still Subdued
items.idea: April US industrial production +0.7% m/m vs +0.3% expected — more than double estimates, strongest since February 2025. Manufacturing +0.62% m/m. Business equipment spending +1.48% m/m, largest since March 2025, to the highest level since March 2019 (7-year high). Defense and space equipment +6.49% y/y for fifth consecutive month. Capacity utilization 76.12% (highest since August). But y/y total IP only +1.35%, still negative in real terms — manufacturing remains 2.4% below its long-run average.
items.angle: Two industrial economies running in parallel: AI/defense cluster booming (business equipment at 7-year highs, defense +6.5% y/y) while the broader complex stagnates in real terms. The AI infrastructure buildout is pulling the IP headline up — strip it out and the picture looks much flatter.
items.tweetA: April IP +0.7% vs +0.3% expected. Business equipment spending at a 7-year high. Defense and space up 6.49% y/y for the 5th consecutive month. The AI infrastructure and defense capex boom is now clearly visible in the hard industrial data.
items.tweetB: Two manufacturing economies: business equipment at 2019 highs, autos up 3.7%, GenAI electronics booming. And total IP year-over-year is +1.35% — below inflation in real terms. The AI and defense cluster is carrying the entire US industrial complex. Strip it out and the picture looks very different.

charts.desc: Charts inside article — Goldman component-level breakdown. Worth opening.

items.title: Akash BME Activation: 53K AKT Burned in 9 Days — Leases +27% QoQ, Revenue -45% QoQ
items.idea: Akash Network's Burn-Mint Equilibrium (BME) activated March 23, 2026 via Mainnet 17, with 53,520 AKT burned in the first 9 days. New leases grew 27.1% QoQ (third consecutive quarter of sequential growth). But lease revenue compressed 45% QoQ as tenants rotated into smaller, lower-cost GPU deployments. Average active providers fell 8.4% QoQ to 58. AKT closed Q1 at $0.50, up 41.6% QoQ, rally concentrated around BME activation date.
items.angle: The 45% revenue compression despite +27% lease growth is the key tension: the market is paying up for usage metrics while unit economics deteriorate. Token +42% on the back of declining revenue is the classic DePIN tension — adoption narrative vs. financial reality.
items.tweetA: Akash: new leases +27% QoQ, third consecutive quarter of sequential growth. Revenue -45% QoQ. Token up 42% QoQ. That is literally the opposite of what you want — rising usage counts, collapsing revenue, rising token price. The decentralized compute narrative is running ahead of the financials.
items.tweetB: 53,520 AKT burned in the first 9 days of BME. That's the headline. Fine print: lease revenue compressed 45% QoQ and average providers fell 8.4%. BME creates deflationary pressure — but only if demand-side activity grows faster than provider supply shrinks.

charts.desc: Charts in full Messari report — quarterly trends on leases, revenue, provider counts.

items.title: ARDC: 10.5% Yield, -5.2% NAV Discount — Ares' $622B Credit Platform Priced at Fear
items.idea: Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (NYSE: ARDC) trading at -5.2% discount to NAV, yielding ~10.5% at market price. Portfolio: 63.2% floating rate, 1.24-year effective duration, 40.64% leverage, 8.44% avg coupon, 297 holdings. Managed by Ares Management ($622B AUM, 560+ credit professionals). 52-week range: +1.64% premium to -11.18% discount. Current -5.2% is slightly below the -4.57% 52-week average discount. YTD 2026: NAV -4.54%, price -6.14%.
items.angle: The floating rate exposure (63.2%) means ARDC structurally benefits from higher-for-longer rates. Buying a -5.2% NAV discount on a $622B credit manager's platform is a fear trade, not a fundamental problem. Key risk: 40.64% leverage amplifies any credit quality deterioration.
items.tweetA: ARDC: managed by the firm running $622B in credit. Yielding 10.5%. Trading at -5.2% discount to NAV. Traded at premium as recently as this year. 63% floating rate. If you are worried about rates staying high — this fund is structurally built for exactly that environment.
items.tweetB: Credit markets pricing fear: ARDC at -5.2% NAV discount. But 63% floating rate and 1.24yr effective duration means rising rates are a tailwind, not a headwind. The irony: the thing scaring investors is what benefits this portfolio. The discount is the opportunity, not the warning.

charts.desc: ARDC Premium/Discount to NAV chart 2025-2026 from CEFConnect — shows widening from +1.64% premium to current -5.2%.

items.title: Citadel's $800K Garden Leave Trap — How Quant Firms Enforce Non-Competes Without Lawyers
items.idea: Young and Calculated details garden leave enforcement at major quant firms. Citadel is most aggressive: cease-and-desist within days of resignation, injunction requests, dedicated legal team monitoring LinkedIn, conference attendance, and competitor hiring announcements. Key insight: 'if you have $800,000 in unvested deferred compensation and a 12-month non-compete, the cost of violating is $800,000 — that is the enforcement mechanism, not the threat of a lawsuit.' Jane Street: strict on paper, pragmatic in enforcement. Code exfiltration creates criminal exposure under the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act.
items.angle: The $800K deferred comp trap is the key structural insight: firms do not need courts to enforce non-competes because they engineered compensation so that breaking the contract costs more than fighting it. This is contract design as surveillance and retention architecture.
items.tweetA: Citadel employs a dedicated legal team that monitors LinkedIn updates and conference attendance of departing employees. The actual enforcement mechanism is not the lawsuit. It is $800K in unvested deferred compensation. You do not need courts when you have already engineered the incentives correctly.
items.tweetB: Quant firm garden leave reality: 6-12 months for senior PMs, up to 24 months combined restriction. Copy one line of code to a personal machine before you leave: federal criminal exposure under the Defend Trade Secrets Act. Not a civil suit. Criminal. That is what the alpha labor market looks like.

charts.desc: No charts — legal and structural analysis.

items.title: Agentic Supply Chain Teaser: 70% Control Layer Market Share, 5-10x Substrate, Two Assemblers
items.idea: Market Sentiment is publishing an Agentic AI basket report (Saturday, paid subscribers). Prior baskets: Optics +80% vs QQQ +15% (Dec 2025), Advanced Packaging +69.9% vs QQQ +15% (Jan 2026). Key teasers: one company has 70% global market share on 'the control layer that boots the machine, manages power, translates signals between CPU and memory' (likely Aspeed Technology — BMC/IPMI chips); AI servers use 5-10x more substrate than traditional servers, fewer than 10 firms worldwide can produce them; two companies physically assemble every AI server for AWS, Microsoft, Meta, Oracle.
items.angle: The server control layer (BMC chips) is one of the least-discussed AI infrastructure choke points. Aspeed Technology has dominant share here and is nearly invisible in Western coverage. If this is the call, it is a name that has been quietly compounding on every AI server unit shipped globally.
items.tweetA: Market Sentiment's prior baskets: Optics +80%, Advanced Packaging +70% vs QQQ. Next call: Agentic AI supply chain. The hook — one company has 70% global market share on the control layer that boots and manages every AI server. AI servers use 5-10x more substrate. Two firms assemble for all hyperscalers.
items.tweetB: Everyone owns NVDA. Almost nobody owns the control layer that manages every AI server in every data center globally. Market Sentiment says one company has 70% world market share on that layer. Their prior baskets outperformed QQQ by 65 percentage points each. Full report Saturday.

charts.desc: No charts in teaser — full report with basket constituents coming Saturday.

items.title: BOLSY: Brazil's Exchange Operator After EWZ Returned 38-47% From Prior Picks
items.idea: Haymaker's Friday POW! introduces Brasil Bolsa Balcão (BOLSY ADR) — the operator of the Brazilian stock exchange — as a new pick. Context: prior EWZ endorsements returned +38.03% and +47.01% from entry points. BOLSY owns the exchange infrastructure that captures trading volume commissions regardless of which individual stocks win or lose. Brazil bull case: net oil exporter, fiscal reform progress, undervaluation vs. developed markets.
items.angle: Moving from EWZ to BOLSY is a classic picks-and-shovels upgrade: if Brazil's equity bull run continues, the exchange operator earns on every trade regardless of direction. Exchange operators are natural leverage plays on market activity growth.
items.tweetA: Haymaker's prior Brazil ETF picks returned +38% and +47%. New call: BOLSY — the exchange operator that runs those markets. Classic picks-and-shovels: if Brazilian equities keep running, the exchange makes money from everyone who trades them, regardless of who is right.
items.tweetB: Brazil bull case: net oil exporter at $100 oil, fiscal reform momentum, discount to EM peers. Haymaker already captured +38-47% on EWZ. The next step up the value chain is BOLSY — the exchange itself. Higher trading volumes benefit the infrastructure, not just the stocks. That's the trade.

charts.desc: Article continues behind paywall — likely includes valuation analysis.

items.title: SPY 8 EMA Bounce Despite Oil Over $100 and Hottest PPI in Years — Flow Beats Fundamentals
items.idea: SPY gapped down hard today but bounced off the 8-day EMA — a 'textbook touch and bounce' per Bulltard. The market is absorbing the hottest PPI print in years and oil over $100 simultaneously without breaking. Bulltard's conclusion: 'Nothing really matters just the flow of money — there are simply more buyers than sellers.' Key technical level: a close below the 8 EMA would target the 21 EMA at 'just over 720.'
items.angle: The market absorbing oil at $100+ and a hot PPI without breaking a key technical level is a flow and positioning story, not a fundamental one. When CTAs hit max long and dealer gamma flips, the 8 EMA will not provide the same cushion. The stop is 720.
items.tweetA: Oil over $100. Hottest PPI in years. US-China summit disappointment. SPY gaps down — then bounces off the 8 EMA. Bulltard: 'More buyers than sellers.' My question: when CTAs hit max long and dealer gamma flips negative, what does the 8 EMA do for you?
items.tweetB: The market is absorbing oil at $100+ and a hot PPI print without breaking. That is either incredible resilience or the last gasp before positioning forces a reset. Watch the close: if SPY closes below the 8 EMA, Bulltard says the next stop is 720 on the 21 EMA.

charts.desc: SPY chart with 8 EMA showing today's gap-down and bounce.
